abstract:
Neuromorphic computing has come to refer to a variety of brain-inspired
computers, devices, and models that contrast the pervasive von Neumann computer
architecture. This biologically inspired approach has created highly connected
synthetic neurons and synapses that can be used to model neuroscience theories
as well as solve challenging machine learning problems. The promise of the
technology is to create a brain-like ability to learn and adapt, but the
technical challenges are significant, starting with an accurate neuroscience
model of how the brain works, to finding materials and engineering
breakthroughs to build devices to support these models, to creating a
programming framework so the systems can learn, to creating applications with
brain-like capabilities. In this work, we provide a comprehensive survey of the
research and motivations for neuromorphic computing over its history. We begin
with a 35-year review of the motivations and drivers of neuromorphic computing,
then look at the major research areas of the field, which we define as
neuro-inspired models, algorithms and learning approaches, hardware and
devices, supporting systems, and finally applications. We conclude with a broad
discussion on the major research topics that need to be addressed in the coming
years to see the promise of neuromorphic computing fulfilled. The goals of this
work are to provide an exhaustive review of the research conducted in
neuromorphic computing since the inception of the term, and to motivate further
work by illuminating gaps in the field where new research is needed.
title: A Survey of Neuromorphic Computing and Neural Networks in Hardware

abstract:
Artificial Intelligence has made remarkable advancements in recent years,
primarily driven by increasingly large deep learning models. However, achieving
true Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) demands fundamentally new
architectures rather than merely scaling up existing models. Current approaches
largely depend on expanding model parameters, which improves task-specific
performance but falls short in enabling continuous, adaptable, and generalized
learning. Achieving AGI capable of continuous learning and personalization on
resource-constrained edge devices is an even bigger challenge.
This paper reviews the state of continual learning and neuroscience-inspired
AI, and proposes a novel architecture for Personalized AGI that integrates
brain-like learning mechanisms for edge deployment. We review literature on
continuous lifelong learning, catastrophic forgetting, and edge AI, and discuss
key neuroscience principles of human learning, including Synaptic Pruning,
Hebbian plasticity, Sparse Coding, and Dual Memory Systems, as inspirations for
AI systems. Building on these insights, we outline an AI architecture that
features complementary fast-and-slow learning modules, synaptic
self-optimization, and memory-efficient model updates to support on-device
lifelong adaptation.
Conceptual diagrams of the proposed architecture and learning processes are
provided. We address challenges such as catastrophic forgetting, memory
efficiency, and system scalability, and present application scenarios for
mobile AI assistants and embodied AI systems like humanoid robots. We conclude
with key takeaways and future research directions toward truly continual,
personalized AGI on the edge. While the architecture is theoretical, it
synthesizes diverse findings and offers a roadmap for future implementation.

keyInsights: Biological principles like synaptic pruning and dual memory systems can inform more efficient AI architectures
Local learning rules enable rapid adaptation while global updates preserve system-wide optimization
Protecting core knowledge in permanent memory while allowing personalization in faster-changing memory tiers
originalProblem: Current AI systems lack the ability to continuously learn and adapt to individual users while maintaining core capabilities
Edge deployment of adaptive AI systems is constrained by limited compute resources and energy efficiency requirements
Catastrophic forgetting prevents effective continuous learning in neural networks
results: Theoretical framework combining neuroscience principles with practical edge AI constraints
Detailed architectural proposal for continuous learning while preventing catastrophic forgetting
Roadmap for implementing personalized AI systems that can adapt to individual users
No experimental validation yet as this is a theoretical proposal
solution: Three-tiered memory architecture with distinct short-term, long-term and permanent memory components
Integration of Hebbian-like local updates for fast learning with periodic gradient optimization
Resource-efficient model compression techniques combined with brain-inspired sparse coding
paper_summary.summary: A neuroscience-inspired architecture combines tri-memory systems (short-term, long-term, and permanent memory) with Hebbian learning principles to enable continuous learning and personalization in edge AI systems, drawing from biological mechanisms like synaptic pruning and sparse coding to address catastrophic forgetting while maintaining resource efficiency.

title: Personalized Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) via  Neuroscience-Inspired Continuous Learning Systems

abstract:
As Large Language Models (LLMs) evolve from text-completion tools into fully
fledged agents operating in dynamic environments, they must address the
challenge of continually learning and retaining long-term knowledge. Many
biological systems solve these challenges with episodic memory, which supports
single-shot learning of instance-specific contexts. Inspired by this, we
present an episodic memory framework for LLM agents, centered around five key
properties of episodic memory that underlie adaptive and context-sensitive
behavior. With various research efforts already partially covering these
properties, this position paper argues that now is the right time for an
explicit, integrated focus on episodic memory to catalyze the development of
long-term agents. To this end, we outline a roadmap that unites several
research directions under the goal to support all five properties of episodic
memory for more efficient long-term LLM agents.

organization_info.name: Max Planck Institute for Software Systems

keyInsights: No single current LLM memory approach adequately provides the full range of capabilities required for truly long-term, adaptive agentic behavior.
Episodic memory, as defined by its five properties, is the essential "missing piece" that can unify and extend fragmented memory solutions in LLM agents.

A biologically-inspired episodic memory system can enable efficient continuous learning, overcome catastrophic forgetting, and provide deep contextual understanding by integrating fast, instance-specific learning with slow, generalized knowledge consolidation.
originalProblem: Current LLM memory architectures (in-context, external, parametric) are fragmented and insufficient for long-term, continuous learning and knowledge retention in autonomous agents.
LLM agents lack the ability to acquire new information and skills over their lifetime, retain knowledge without degradation, and act based on specific past contextual events.
Existing memory solutions struggle to balance high computational costs for long contexts with the need for instance-specific, contextualized, and continuously evolving knowledge.
results: Analytically demonstrates that existing LLM memory paradigms (in-context, external, parametric) each fall short on critical properties of episodic memory, highlighting their inherent limitations.
Introduces a conceptual architectural framework for LLM agents incorporating episodic memory, illustrating its interaction with other memory types for encoding, retrieval, and consolidation.
Outlines a research roadmap with six specific questions across encoding, retrieval, consolidation, and benchmarking to guide the development of episodic memory systems for LLM agents.
solution: Proposes an explicit, integrated episodic memory system for LLM agents, inspired by the biological Complementary Learning Systems Theory.
Defines episodic memory for LLMs by five key properties: long-term storage, explicit reasoning, single-shot learning, instance-specificity, and contextual relations.
Presents a unifying conceptual framework that integrates in-context, external, and parametric memory components through processes of encoding, retrieval, and consolidation.
paper_summary.summary: This position paper argues that an integrated episodic memory system is essential for developing large language model (LLM) agents capable of sustained, adaptive operation over long periods. It proposes a biologically-inspired framework to unify existing memory approaches, enabling continuous learning and context-sensitive knowledge retention.

title: Position: Episodic Memory is the Missing Piece for Long-Term LLM Agents

abstract: Foundation models have transformed machine learning through large-scale pretraining and increased test-time compute. Despite surpassing human performance in several domains, these models remain fundamentally limited in continuous operation, experience accumulation, and personalization, capabilities that are central to adaptive intelligence. While continual learning research has long targeted these goals, its historical focus on in-weight learning (IWL), i.e., updating a single model's parameters to absorb new knowledge, has rendered catastrophic forgetting a persistent challenge. Our position is that combining the strengths of In-Weight Learning (IWL) and the newly emerged capabilities of In-Context Learning (ICL) through the design of modular memory is the missing piece for continual adaptation at scale. We outline a conceptual framework for modular memory-centric architectures that leverage ICL for rapid adaptation and knowledge accumulation, and IWL for stable updates to model capabilities, charting a practical roadmap toward continually learning agents.

keyInsights: Neither In-Weight Learning nor In-Context Learning alone is sufficient for scalable and robust continual adaptation; a modular integration of both is essential.
Biological and computer memory systems offer critical inspiration, emphasizing modularity, multiple timescales, and active management for adaptive intelligence.

Different memory representations (slot-based versus distributed neural) present distinct trade-offs in terms of efficiency, capacity, interference, and retrieval, necessitating careful design choices.
originalProblem: Foundation Models (FMs) lack the ability to continuously operate, accumulate diverse experiences, or personalize effectively over time.
Traditional Continual Learning (CL) models, primarily relying on In-Weight Learning (IWL), suffer from catastrophic forgetting and struggle with the stability-plasticity dilemma.
In-Context Learning (ICL) in FMs faces limitations such as computational inefficiency with growing context lengths and an inability for the core model to adapt to fundamental distribution shifts.
results: The paper presents a detailed conceptual framework for continual learning agents with a core model, working memory, and long-term memory, specifying their roles and interactions.
Operational regimes for external interaction and internal consolidation are delineated, demonstrating how rapid ICL-based adaptation and stable IWL-based knowledge refinement can coexist.
An analytical assessment of memory representations outlines their strengths and weaknesses against key desiderata, providing guidance for developing future modular memory systems.
solution: A Modular Memory Framework is proposed that synergistically integrates IWL and ICL for continuous adaptation.

The framework defines three interacting components: a Core Model (updated via IWL), a Working Memory (for transient context and ICL), and a Long-Term Memory (for persistent experience storage and ICL retrieval).

Two distinct operational regimes are established: an External Interaction Regime for real-time responsiveness and an Internal Consolidation Regime for sparse, stable knowledge distillation into the core model.
paper_summary.summary: A conceptual framework outlines a modular memory system designed to integrate In-Weight Learning and In-Context Learning, enabling AI agents to achieve robust and scalable continual adaptation. This framework provides a comprehensive blueprint for addressing the limitations of current foundation models and traditional continual learning.

title: Modular Memory is the Key to Continual Learning Agents

abstract: Artificial intelligence has advanced rapidly in the last decade, driven primarily by progress in the scale of deep-learning systems. Despite these advances, the creation of intelligent systems that can operate effectively in diverse, real-world environments remains a significant challenge. In this white paper, we outline the Thousand Brains Project, an ongoing research effort to develop an alternative, complementary form of AI, derived from the operating principles of the neocortex. We present an early version of a thousand-brains system, a sensorimotor agent that is uniquely suited to quickly learn a wide range of tasks and eventually implement any capabilities the human neocortex has. Core to its design is the use of a repeating computational unit, the learning module, modeled on the cortical columns found in mammalian brains. Each learning module operates as a semi-independent unit that can model entire objects, represents information through spatially structured reference frames, and both estimates and is able to effect movement in the world. Learning is a quick, associative process, similar to Hebbian learning in the brain, and leverages inductive biases around the spatial structure of the world to enable rapid and continual learning. Multiple learning modules can interact with one another both hierarchically and non-hierarchically via a "cortical messaging protocol" (CMP), creating more abstract representations and supporting multimodal integration. We outline the key principles motivating the design of thousand-brains systems and provide details about the implementation of Monty, our first instantiation of such a system. Code can be found at this https URL, along with more detailed documentation at this https URL.

keyInsights: Intelligence emerges from sensorimotor interactions where the neocortex builds numerous complete models of objects and their parts in parallel, each within its own reference frame.
A universal 'Cortical Messaging Protocol' (CMP) based on 'feature-at-a-pose' allows for modular, scalable, and modality-agnostic communication between computational units.
The cortical column, replicated as a 'Learning Module,' functions as a repeating, semi-independent unit capable of learning structured, graph-based object models and generating motor commands.
originalProblem: Current AI, particularly deep learning, struggles with effective operation in diverse, real-world environments due to reliance on static datasets and lack of explicit structured world models.
Existing AI systems often require massive, expensive training datasets and face challenges with continual learning and catastrophic forgetting.
Many deep learning models lack interpretability and struggle with precise spatial reasoning, manipulation, and generalization from small samples.
results: Successfully presented a detailed architectural blueprint for Monty, an initial sensorimotor AI system based on the Thousand Brains Theory, defining core components and their interactions.
Established the Cortical Messaging Protocol (CMP) as a viable universal communication standard, enabling flexible and scalable integration of heterogeneous modules.
Released the Monty codebase and documentation as an open-source SDK, providing an accessible foundation for further research and development in this new AI paradigm.
solution: Developing an alternative AI paradigm that operationalizes the 'Thousand Brains Theory of Intelligence' by using 'Learning Modules' (inspired by cortical columns) as the core computational units.
Implementing sensorimotor learning as foundational, where intelligence arises from continuous interaction and movement within an environment, with ubiquitous motor outputs at all processing levels.
Building explicit, structured world models using internal 'reference frames' and graph-based representations to enable rapid, continual, and unsupervised learning from small samples.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Numenta, Inc., are developing a biologically-inspired AI system called 'The Thousand Brains Project,' which models intelligence based on the neocortex's principles of building multiple object models in parallel. Their initial implementation, Monty, uses modular 'Learning Modules' that learn structured, spatial world models from sensorimotor input, communicating via a universal 'Cortical Messaging Protocol' to enable rapid, continual learning and interaction in complex environments.

title: The Thousand Brains Project: A New Paradigm for Sensorimotor Intelligence

abstract:
This review motivates and synthesizes research efforts in
neuroscience-inspired artificial intelligence and biomimetic computing in terms
of mortal computation. Specifically, we characterize the notion of mortality by
recasting ideas in biophysics, cybernetics, and cognitive science in terms of a
theoretical foundation for sentient behavior. We frame the mortal computation
thesis through the Markov blanket formalism and the circular causality entailed
by inference, learning, and selection. The ensuing framework -- underwritten by
the free energy principle -- could prove useful for guiding the construction of
unconventional connectionist computational systems, neuromorphic intelligence,
and chimeric agents, including sentient organoids, which stand to revolutionize
the long-term future of embodied, enactive artificial intelligence and
cognition research.

keyInsights: Intelligence arises from an intrinsic drive for self-preservation and persistence, which necessitates an implicit understanding of finitude.
Computation in biological systems is inherently "morphic," where physical morphology and computational processes are inseparable and co-designed.
Adaptive behavior in "mortal computers" emerges from a strong circular causality between fast inference, slower learning, and the slowest structural selection processes, all minimizing free energy.
originalProblem: Prevailing "immortal computation" paradigms are thermodynamically inefficient and fundamentally limit the kind of adaptive intelligence observed in biological systems.

Current AI approaches lack the intrinsic drive for self-preservation, generalized adaptability, and resilience characteristic of biological intelligence, hindering the path to Artificial General Intelligence (AGI).
Intelligence is often abstracted from its physical substrate and environmental interaction, overlooking the essential role of embodiment and physical form in cognition.
results: A five-point informal definition of a "mortal computer" is established, emphasizing its morphic morphology, primary imperatives for persistence, generalized homeostasis, intertwined inference-learning-selection (MILS), and co-designed computation with its substrate.

Three classes of mortal computers are proposed: Homeostatic, Allostatic, and Autopoietic, with the Autopoietic class representing the most advanced, self-organizing, and potentially AGI-capable systems due to their self-maintenance and self-replication.

Existing biological and synthetic systems like Ashby's Homeostat, Xenobots, fungal computing, and organoid intelligence are presented as examples that embody various aspects of the mortal computation framework.
solution: Proposes "mortal computation," a paradigm where computational processes are intrinsically tied to their physical substrate and the imperative of persistence, contrasting it with traditional "immortal computation."

Synthesizes theoretical concepts from biophysics (e.g., autopoiesis, thermodynamics), cybernetics (e.g., self-organization, requisite variety), and cognitive science (e.g., embodied cognition) to build a unified foundation.

Utilizes Karl Friston's Free Energy Principle and the Markov blanket formalism as the unifying mathematical framework to describe how systems maintain their integrity and adapt through inference, learning, and structural selection.
paper_summary.summary: Alexander Ororbia and Karl Friston introduce "mortal computation" as a conceptual framework for developing biomimetic intelligence, integrating principles from biophysics, cybernetics, and cognitive science under the Free Energy Principle. This framework posits that intelligence arises from an intrinsic drive for self-preservation, offering a pathway to Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) that is both robust and energy-efficient by tightly binding computation to its physical substrate.

title: Mortal Computation: A Foundation for Biomimetic Intelligence

abstract:
We re-evaluate universal computation based on the synthesis of Turing
machines. This leads to a view of programs as singularities of analytic
varieties or, equivalently, as phases of the Bayesian posterior of a synthesis
problem. This new point of view reveals unexplored directions of research in
program synthesis, of which neural networks are a subset, for example in
relation to phase transitions, complexity and generalisation. We also lay the
empirical foundations for these new directions by reporting on our
implementation in code of some simple experiments.
title: Geometry of Program Synthesis

abstract: We propose a set of precise criteria for saying a neural net learns and uses a "world model." The goal is to give an operational meaning to terms that are often used informally, in order to provide a common language for experimental investigation. We focus specifically on the idea of representing a latent "state space" of the world, leaving modeling the effect of actions to future work. Our definition is based on ideas from the linear probing literature, and formalizes the notion of a computation that factors through a representation of the data generation process. An essential addition to the definition is a set of conditions to check that such a "world model" is not a trivial consequence of the neural net's data or task.

keyInsights: A "world model" in a neural network can be rigorously defined by a precise mathematical relationship between the real world, observed input, internal states, and a simplified world representation, projected via "simple" functions.
Crucially, a valid "world model" must be genuinely "learned" (not merely present in inputs) and "emergent" (not trivially derived from outputs) through the network's internal processing.
Identifying "causal world models" allows researchers to pinpoint internal representations that directly and predictably influence specific aspects of a network's output behavior.
originalProblem: Informal and inconsistent usage of "world model" in AI leads to ambiguity, misunderstandings, and hinders scientific progress.
Lack of precise, testable criteria makes it difficult to ascertain if neural networks genuinely form internal representations beyond external behavior.
Debates about AI "understanding" often lack a rigorous framework for empirical investigation of internal mechanisms.
results: A formal, operational framework defining a "world model" in neural networks, including conditions for it to be considered "learned," "emergent," and "causal."

The framework specifies that mapping functions from internal states to the world model must be "simple" (e.g., linear) to prevent trivial interpretations, aligning theoretical definitions with practical probing methodologies.

Specific definitions for "complete causal" and "partial causal" world models are provided, enabling the identification of internal representations that govern particular output behaviors, moving beyond purely correlational findings.
solution: Proposing a formal, mathematical definition of a "world model" in neural network internal states, utilizing commutative diagrams to capture relationships between the world, observations, and internal representations.

Stipulating that the mapping functions from internal states to the world model must be "simple" (e.g., linear) to ensure the definition is nontrivial and testable, aligning with practical probing methods.
Introducing explicit conditions to define "learned" and "emergent" world models, ensuring they are not trivially derivable from input data or the network's explicit output task.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Harvard University introduce a formal, operational definition for a "world model" within neural networks, providing precise, testable criteria to determine if a network's internal states represent simplified aspects of the real world, and distinguishing truly learned and emergent models from trivial correlations.

title: What Does it Mean for a Neural Network to Learn a "World Model"?

abstract: This position and survey paper identifies the emerging convergence of neuroscience, artificial general intelligence (AGI), and neuromorphic computing toward a unified research paradigm. Using a framework grounded in brain physiology, we highlight how synaptic plasticity, sparse spike-based communication, and multimodal association provide design principles for next-generation AGI systems that potentially combine both human and machine intelligences. The review traces this evolution from early connectionist models to state-of-the-art large language models, demonstrating how key innovations like transformer attention, foundation-model pre-training, and multi-agent architectures mirror neurobiological processes like cortical mechanisms, working memory, and episodic consolidation. We then discuss emerging physical substrates capable of breaking the von Neumann bottleneck to achieve brain-scale efficiency in silicon: memristive crossbars, in-memory compute arrays, and emerging quantum and photonic devices. There are four critical challenges at this intersection: 1) integrating spiking dynamics with foundation models, 2) maintaining lifelong plasticity without catastrophic forgetting, 3) unifying language with sensorimotor learning in embodied agents, and 4) enforcing ethical safeguards in advanced neuromorphic autonomous systems. This combined perspective across neuroscience, computation, and hardware offers an integrative agenda for in each of these fields.

keyInsights: Neuroscience, AGI, and neuromorphic computing are converging into a unified research paradigm, driven by the need for more efficient and adaptable intelligence.
Key innovations in modern AI, such as transformer attention and foundation models, increasingly reflect neurobiological principles.
Specialized physical substrates, like memristive crossbars and quantum devices, are essential to achieve brain-scale energy efficiency by overcoming traditional computing bottlenecks.
originalProblem: Artificial intelligence, neuroscience, and neuromorphic computing often operate in silos, hindering synergistic progress.
Current AI models face limitations in energy efficiency, adaptability, and achieving true artificial general intelligence.
Conventional computing architectures are constrained by the von Neumann bottleneck, impeding brain-scale efficiency.
results: The review synthesizes advancements in connectomics, brain simulations, and neuromodulation, providing biological blueprints for AI.
Current large language models demonstrate emergent capabilities but possess critical limitations in embodiment, causal reasoning, and energy efficiency.
Progress in neuromorphic hardware (e.g., Loihi 2) and spiking neural networks (SNNs) offers paths to competitive performance with enhanced energy efficiency.
solution: Proposing a unified research paradigm that explicitly links insights from brain physiology to AI algorithms and neuromorphic hardware design.
Conducting a comprehensive literature review and synthesis across neuroscience, AGI, and neuromorphic computing domains.
Outlining an integrative research agenda and identifying four critical challenges to guide future interdisciplinary efforts.
paper_summary.summary: A unified research paradigm is outlined for integrating neuroscience, artificial general intelligence (AGI), and neuromorphic computing, synthesizing current advancements across these fields and proposing an agenda to address interdisciplinary challenges.

title: Bridging Brains and Machines: A Unified Frontier in Neuroscience, Artificial Intelligence, and Neuromorphic Systems

abstract:
Machine learning and AI have achieved remarkable advancements but at the cost
of significant computational resources and energy consumption. This has created
an urgent need for a novel, energy-efficient computational fabric to replace
the current computing pipeline. Recently, a promising approach has emerged by
mimicking spiking neurons in the brain and leveraging oscillators on CMOS for
direct computation. In this context, we propose a new and energy efficient
machine learning framework implemented on CMOS Oscillator Networks (OscNet). We
model the developmental processes of the prenatal brain's visual system using
OscNet, updating weights based on the biologically inspired Hebbian rule. This
same pipeline is then directly applied to standard machine learning tasks.
OscNet is a specially designed hardware and is inherently energy-efficient. Its
reliance on forward propagation alone for training further enhances its energy
efficiency while maintaining biological plausibility. Simulation validates our
designs of OscNet architectures. Experimental results demonstrate that Hebbian
learning pipeline on OscNet achieves performance comparable to or even
surpassing traditional machine learning algorithms, highlighting its potential
as a energy efficient and effective computational paradigm.

keyInsights: Computation is performed by the natural physics of coupled oscillators, where the network inherently minimizes a global energy function (Potts Hamiltonian) to find solutions.

The learning mechanism is biologically inspired, using only forward propagation and Hebbian rules, analogous to prenatal brain development, which eliminates the need for energy-intensive backpropagation.
OscNet represents a departure from traditional von Neumann architectures, leveraging an alternative computational paradigm that enables parallel and distributed processing directly in hardware.
originalProblem: Modern AI models face exponentially growing computational demands and high energy consumption.
The traditional CPU-GPU computing paradigm struggles with sustainability and efficiency as machine learning models become more complex.
Existing neural networks heavily rely on energy-intensive backpropagation algorithms for learning.
results: Achieved 57.31% accuracy in unsupervised classification on MNIST, outperforming single-layer (13.77%) and two-layer (28.72%) autoencoders.
Reached 90.58% accuracy in supervised classification on MNIST with 64 hidden neurons, exceeding autoencoder performance (89.96%) with pre-trained features.
Demonstrated comparable clustering performance to traditional K-means on MNIST, achieving 60.84% accuracy compared to 59.46%.
solution: Stanford University researchers developed OscNet, a hardware-based machine learning system utilizing CMOS oscillator networks for computation.
Information is encoded in the phases of coupled oscillators, and the network's 'weights' are implemented as coupling strengths between oscillators, following the Kuramoto model.
The architecture employs a Multi-Input Multi-Output (MIMO) design with input and output layers, performing learning via local, Hebbian-like updates based on a Winner-Takes-All mechanism.
paper_summary.summary: Stanford University's OscNet introduces a machine learning paradigm that leverages CMOS oscillator networks to perform computations directly in hardware, addressing energy efficiency challenges in AI. The system, inspired by biological learning, achieves competitive performance on tasks like MNIST classification, outperforming traditional autoencoders in unsupervised learning by achieving 57.31% accuracy.

title: OscNet: Machine Learning on CMOS Oscillator Networks

abstract:
Autonomous AI systems reveal foundational limitations in deterministic,
human-authored computing architectures. This paper presents Cognitive Silicon:
a hypothetical full-stack architectural framework projected toward 2035,
exploring a possible trajectory for cognitive computing system design. The
proposed architecture would integrate symbolic scaffolding, governed memory,
runtime moral coherence, and alignment-aware execution across
silicon-to-semantics layers. Our design grammar has emerged from dialectical
co-design with LLMs under asymmetric epistemic conditions--creating structured
friction to expose blind spots and trade-offs. The envisioned framework would
establish mortality as a natural consequence of physical constraints,
non-copyable tacit knowledge, and non-cloneable identity keys as
cognitive-embodiment primitives. Core tensions (trust/agency,
scaffolding/emergence, execution/governance) would function as central
architectural pressures rather than edge cases. The architecture theoretically
converges with the Free Energy Principle, potentially offering a formal account
of how cognitive systems could maintain identity through prediction error
minimization across physical and computational boundaries. The resulting
framework aims to deliver a morally tractable cognitive infrastructure that
could maintain human-alignment through irreversible hardware constraints and
identity-bound epistemic mechanisms resistant to replication or subversion.
title: Cognitive Silicon: An Architectural Blueprint for Post-Industrial  Computing Systems

abstract:
As artificial intelligence models have exploded in scale and capability,
understanding of their internal mechanisms remains a critical challenge.
Inspired by the success of dynamical systems approaches in neuroscience, here
we propose a novel framework for studying computations in deep learning
systems. We focus on the residual stream (RS) in transformer models,
conceptualizing it as a dynamical system evolving across layers. We find that
activations of individual RS units exhibit strong continuity across layers,
despite the RS being a non-privileged basis. Activations in the RS accelerate
and grow denser over layers, while individual units trace unstable periodic
orbits. In reduced-dimensional spaces, the RS follows a curved trajectory with
attractor-like dynamics in the lower layers. These insights bridge dynamical
systems theory and mechanistic interpretability, establishing a foundation for
a "neuroscience of AI" that combines theoretical rigor with large-scale data
analysis to advance our understanding of modern neural networks.

keyInsights: Individual residual stream units show strong continuity (r > 0.8) and maintain identity across layers, suggesting persistent computational roles.
Information processing involves a systematic evolution where representations accelerate, activations densify, and mutual information declines while linear correlations increase.

Transformers exhibit structured internal dynamics, including rotational patterns in individual units and self-correcting, attractor-like behavior in mid-layers that return perturbed trajectories to the mean.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) lack transparency regarding their internal operational mechanisms, raising concerns about safety, fairness, and risks.
Current mechanistic interpretability methods provide fragmented insights, often focusing on static, discrete circuits which may be insufficient for understanding complex computations.
A comprehensive framework is needed to move beyond localizable, discrete functional units and understand the dynamic evolution of information in deep neural networks.
results: Individual RS unit activations showed high Pearson correlations (r > 0.8) between successive layers, indicating strong continuity throughout the network.
Mutual information between adjacent layers sharply decreased in early stages, accompanied by increasing linear correlations and activation magnitudes, reflecting a re-encoding of information.
Perturbation experiments revealed robust self-correction in mid-layers, where perturbed trajectories returned to the unperturbed mean, suggesting attractor-like dynamics.
solution: Apply a "neuroscience of AI" framework, viewing the transformer's residual stream (RS) as a dynamic system evolving across layers.
Utilize quantitative methods such as Pearson correlation, mutual information, and L2 norm velocity to characterize RS activation changes.
Employ dimensionality reduction (Compressing Autoencoder, PCA) and perturbation analysis to visualize and test the stability of representational trajectories.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers propose a "neuroscience of AI" framework, applying dynamical systems theory to interpret transformer models by analyzing the residual stream's evolution. This approach uncovers strong continuity in individual unit activations, systematic acceleration and densification of representations, and self-correcting, attractor-like dynamics in reduced-dimensional spaces, especially in the model's mid-layers.

title: Transformer Dynamics: A neuroscientific approach to interpretability of  large language models

abstract:
Contemporary deep learning architectures lack principled means for capturing
and handling fundamental visual concepts, like objects, shapes, geometric
transforms, and other higher-level structures. We propose a neurosymbolic
architecture that uses a domain-specific language to capture selected priors of
image formation, including object shape, appearance, categorization, and
geometric transforms. We express template programs in that language and learn
their parameterization with features extracted from the scene by a
convolutional neural network. When executed, the parameterized program produces
geometric primitives which are rendered and assessed for correspondence with
the scene content and trained via auto-association with gradient. We confront
our approach with a baseline method on a synthetic benchmark and demonstrate
its capacity to disentangle selected aspects of the image formation process,
learn from small data, correct inference in the presence of noise, and
out-of-sample generalization.
title: Disentangling Visual Priors: Unsupervised Learning of Scene  Interpretations with Compositional Autoencoder

abstract:
The Neural Turing Machine (NTM) is more expressive than all previously considered models because of its external memory. It can be viewed as a broader effort to use abstract external Interfaces and to learn a parametric model that interacts with them.
The capabilities of a model can be extended by providing it with proper Interfaces that interact with the world. These external Interfaces include memory, a database, a search engine, or a piece of software such as a theorem verifier. Some of these Interfaces are provided by the developers of the model. However, many important existing Interfaces, such as databases and search engines, are discrete.
We examine feasibility of learning models to interact with discrete Interfaces. We investigate the following discrete Interfaces: a memory Tape, an input Tape, and an output Tape. We use a Reinforcement Learning algorithm to train a neural network that interacts with such Interfaces to solve simple algorithmic tasks. Our Interfaces are expressive enough to make our model Turing complete.
title: Reinforcement Learning Neural Turing Machines - Revised

abstract: Neuromorphic computing seeks to replicate the remarkable efficiency, flexibility, and adaptability of the human brain in artificial systems. Unlike conventional digital approaches, which suffer from the Von Neumann bottleneck and depend on massive computational and energy resources, neuromorphic systems exploit brain-inspired principles of computation to achieve orders of magnitude greater energy efficiency. By drawing on insights from a wide range of disciplines -- including artificial intelligence, physics, chemistry, biology, neuroscience, cognitive science and materials science -- neuromorphic computing promises to deliver intelligent systems that are sustainable, transparent, and widely accessible. A central challenge, however, is to identify a unifying theoretical framework capable of bridging these diverse disciplines. We argue that dynamical systems theory provides such a foundation. Rooted in differential calculus, it offers a principled language for modeling inference, learning, and control in both natural and artificial substrates. Within this framework, noise can be harnessed as a resource for learning, while differential genetic programming enables the discovery of dynamical systems that implement adaptive behaviors. Embracing this perspective paves the way toward emergent neuromorphic intelligence, where intelligent behavior arises from the dynamics of physical substrates, advancing both the science and sustainability of AI.

organization_info.name: Donders Institute for Brain, Cognition, and Behaviour

keyInsights: Intelligent, adaptive behavior can emerge from the intrinsic evolution governed by the physical equations of motion within a neuromorphic agent, across both experience-dependent and evolutionary timescales.
Intrinsic system noise, typically viewed as a detrimental factor, can be effectively leveraged as a computational resource for learning and credit assignment in neuromorphic systems.

Neuromorphic systems enable 'in-physics' computing, where the physical substrate directly implements algorithms, blurring software/hardware distinctions and promising orders of magnitude better energy efficiency.
originalProblem: Current large-scale deep learning models consume vast energy and computational resources, leading to sustainability concerns and a significant carbon footprint.
The resource-intensive nature of modern AI limits accessibility to large corporations and results in opaque, difficult-to-understand 'black box' systems.
Neuromorphic computing lacks a unifying theoretical framework to integrate insights from diverse scientific disciplines like AI, neuroscience, physics, and materials science.
results: Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Adaptation (OUA) successfully enabled a Continuous-Time Recurrent Neural Network (CTRNN) agent to learn adaptive control, leading to a higher cumulative reward and stable particle positioning in simulations.

Differential Genetic Programming (DGP) effectively evolved interpretable symbolic expressions for agent dynamics, demonstrating an increase in maximum fitness over generations for a stochastic control problem.
The paper established DST as a formal language for neuromorphic intelligence, providing a structured approach through Marr's levels for defining and developing brain-inspired AI systems.
solution: Proposing Dynamical Systems Theory (DST) as a common theoretical foundation for neuromorphic computing, spanning computational, algorithmic, and implementational levels of analysis.
Introducing noise-based learning mechanisms, such as Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Adaptation (OUA), that harness intrinsic system noise as a computational resource for online, in-situ adaptation.
Utilizing Differential Genetic Programming (DGP) to automatically discover and evolve symbolic expressions for agent dynamics, enabling a 'bottom-up' approach to emergent intelligence.
paper_summary.summary: Marcel van Gerven's "Neuromorphic Intelligence" introduces a theoretical framework for neuromorphic computing based on dynamical systems theory. This framework offers a principled approach to designing sustainable, transparent, and accessible intelligent systems by leveraging brain-inspired efficiency and demonstrating noise-based learning and evolutionary design.

title: Neuromorphic Intelligence

abstract: We propose Embodied AI as the next fundamental step in the pursuit of Artificial General Intelligence, juxtaposing it against current AI advancements, particularly Large Language Models. We traverse the evolution of the embodiment concept across diverse fields - philosophy, psychology, neuroscience, and robotics - to highlight how EAI distinguishes itself from the classical paradigm of static learning. By broadening the scope of Embodied AI, we introduce a theoretical framework based on cognitive architectures, emphasizing perception, action, memory, and learning as essential components of an embodied agent. This framework is aligned with Friston's active inference principle, offering a comprehensive approach to EAI development. Despite the progress made in the field of AI, substantial challenges, such as the formulation of a novel AI learning theory and the innovation of advanced hardware, persist. Our discussion lays down a foundational guideline for future Embodied AI research. Highlighting the importance of creating Embodied AI agents capable of seamless communication, collaboration, and coexistence with humans and other intelligent entities within real-world environments, we aim to steer the AI community towards addressing the multifaceted challenges and seizing the opportunities that lie ahead in the quest for AGI.

keyInsights: Intelligence, as observed in biological systems, is inherently embodied and develops through active, continuous interaction with the environment.
Embodied AI naturally supports multimodal learning, improves AI alignment through procedural interaction, and enables ongoing evolution in dynamic settings.
Embodiment is a prerequisite for a deeper understanding of cognitive phenomena such as causality, affordances, qualia, and consciousness, which are crucial for true AGI.
originalProblem: Current AI, particularly Large Language Models, are static and unable to evolve or acquire new knowledge from continuous interaction, leading to issues like confabulation and challenges in alignment.
Existing AI struggles with a deep understanding of causality and often lacks the ability to "care about the truth" in a grounded manner.
The dominant paradigm of disembodied AI systems (e.g., Internet AI) does not fully align with how biological intelligence develops through active interaction with the world.
results: Current Internet AI, especially Large Language Models, are fundamentally static, struggle with confabulation and causality, and are insufficient for achieving Artificial General Intelligence.
Embodied AI (E-AI) is presented as the essential next step towards AGI, enabling agents to dynamically interact with and learn from the real world, leading to improved understanding and alignment.
A comprehensive theoretical framework for E-AI is proposed, detailing its core components of Perception, Action, Memory, and Learning, all aligned with the Active Inference Principle.
solution: Advocates for Embodied AI (E-AI) as the necessary approach, where agents are bound to, observe, interact with, and learn from the real world continuously.
Proposes a theoretical framework for E-AI systems built upon cognitive architectures, integrating machine learning methods for its four essential components: Perception, Action, Memory, and Learning.
Aligns this framework explicitly with Karl Friston's Active Inference Principle, suggesting agents minimize surprise by actively changing their environment.
paper_summary.summary: The paper advocates for Embodied AI (E-AI) as the crucial next frontier for achieving Artificial General Intelligence, arguing that current static AI models like Large Language Models lack grounded understanding, causality, and dynamic adaptation. It proposes a theoretical framework for E-AI comprising perception, action, memory, and learning, aligning these components with the active inference principle to enable continuous interaction with the world.

title: A call for embodied AI

abstract: Artificial intelligence has advanced significantly through deep learning, reinforcement learning, and large language and vision models. However, these systems often remain task specific, struggle to adapt to changing conditions, and cannot generalize in ways similar to human cognition. Additionally, they mainly focus on mimicking brain structures, which often leads to black-box models with limited transparency and adaptability. Inspired by the structure and function of biological cognition, this paper introduces the concept of "Neurocognitive-Inspired Intelligence (NII)," a hybrid approach that combines neuroscience, cognitive science, computer vision, and AI to develop more general, adaptive, and robust intelligent systems capable of rapid learning, learning from less data, and leveraging prior experience. These systems aim to emulate the human brain's ability to flexibly learn, reason, remember, perceive, and act in real-world settings with minimal supervision. We review the limitations of current AI methods, define core principles of neurocognitive-inspired intelligence, and propose a modular, biologically inspired architecture that emphasizes integration, embodiment, and adaptability. We also discuss potential implementation strategies and outline various real-world applications, from robotics to education and healthcare. Importantly, this paper offers a hybrid roadmap for future research, laying the groundwork for building AI systems that more closely resemble human cognition.

keyInsights: Moving beyond structural brain mimicry to functional emulation of cognitive processes can overcome fundamental limitations of current AI, leading to more general and adaptable intelligence.

A modular, hierarchical, and closed-loop cognitive architecture, integrating perception, attention, memory, and reasoning, is essential for robust, context-aware, and continuously learning AI systems.

Multimodal integration, dynamic adaptation, and an emphasis on embodied cognition are crucial for creating AI that can effectively operate and generalize in complex, uncertain real-world environments.
originalProblem: Current AI systems are task-specific, exhibiting poor generalization and struggling to adapt to novel or out-of-distribution scenarios.
Existing AI models are data-inefficient, heavily relying on vast labeled datasets, contrasting with human few-shot learning capabilities.
Deficiencies in core cognitive functions like temporal memory, causal understanding, common sense reasoning, and inherent interpretability limit current AI's human-like intelligence.
results: A proof-of-concept experiment demonstrated that dynamically tracking incremental pixel-level adversarial perturbations could inform adaptive classification, suggesting an NII system's capacity for context-sensitive learning.

Conceptual experiments showed that multimodal fusion, combining tactile and visual inputs, achieved 97.26% accuracy in surface material classification, outperforming vision-only (87.88%) and tactile-only (92.67%) models.

These demonstrations illustrate that adopting neurocognitive principles can lead to AI systems that are more robust, adaptive, and capable of operating effectively in dynamic and uncertain real-world settings.
solution: The Neurocognitive-Inspired Intelligence (NII) framework is introduced, a hybrid, interdisciplinary approach integrating insights from neuroscience and cognitive science.

NII is designed as a modular, hierarchical, and closed-loop cognitive architecture comprising seven biologically inspired units: Perception, Attention, Memory, Learning, Reasoning, Adaptation, and Action.

The framework emphasizes replicating the dynamic, functional aspects of cognition, such as active perception, dual-memory systems (working and long-term), and a hybrid reasoning engine, rather than merely mimicking brain structures.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at The University of Alabama propose the Neurocognitive-Inspired Intelligence (NII) framework, moving beyond structural mimicry to functional emulation of human cognition. This conceptual framework aims to develop more general, adaptive, and robust AI systems capable of human-like learning and reasoning.

title: Towards Neurocognitive-Inspired Intelligence: From AI's Structural Mimicry to Human-Like Functional Cognition

abstract:
The acceleration race of digital computing technologies seems to be steering
toward impasses -- technological, economical and environmental -- a condition
that has spurred research efforts in alternative, "neuromorphic" (brain-like)
computing technologies. Furthermore, since decades the idea of exploiting
nonlinear physical phenomena "directly" for non-digital computing has been
explored under names like "unconventional computing", "natural computing",
"physical computing", or "in-materio computing". This has been taking place in
niches which are small compared to other sectors of computer science. In this
paper I stake out the grounds of how a general concept of "computing" can be
developed which comprises digital, neuromorphic, unconventional and possible
future "computing" paradigms. The main contribution of this paper is a
wide-scope survey of existing formal conceptualizations of "computing". The
survey inspects approaches rooted in three different kinds of background
mathematics: discrete-symbolic formalisms, probabilistic modeling, and
dynamical-systems oriented views. It turns out that different choices of
background mathematics lead to decisively different understandings of what
"computing" is. Across all of this diversity, a unifying coordinate system for
theorizing about "computing" can be distilled. Within these coordinates I
locate anchor points for a foundational formal theory of a future
computing-engineering discipline that includes, but will reach beyond, digital
and neuromorphic computing.

keyInsights: The choice of mathematical formalism profoundly shapes the conceptualization of "computing," with logic-based, probabilistic, and dynamical systems approaches exhibiting strikingly different operational principles for time, semantics, and compositionality.

While digital computing has a unified and mature theory, probabilistic computing has a unified 'what-formalism' but diverse 'how-formalisms,' and dynamical systems lack a comprehensive 'what-theory' due to the open-ended nature of qualitative phenomena.

A generalized theory of computing, particularly "Modal Computing," requires a "profoundly new mathematical language" to capture "gradual qualitative change," transcending the limitations of existing mathematical tools from logic, probability, and dynamical systems.
originalProblem: Digital computing faces growing impasses including an escalating energy footprint, physical limits to miniaturization, increasing toxic waste, and a persistent 'software crisis'.
Neuromorphic and unconventional computing paradigms show promise but lack a unifying theoretical foundation, leading to fragmentation and hindering broad progress.
A narrow, Turing-centric definition of computing limits the exploration and principled exploitation of diverse physical phenomena and materials for computational tasks.
results: Digital computing offers unified, mature hierarchies of formalisms (how-models based on expressiveness, what-models based on semantic expressiveness), well-defined formal semantics, discrete time, and static-syntactic compositionality.

Probabilistic computing features a singular 'what-formalism' (probability theory), but diverse 'how-formalisms' lacking systematic interrelationships, defines time as crucial for probability distribution growth (smeared over time), and lacks clear 'syntactic' compositionality.

Dynamical systems-oriented computing presents a unified 'how-formalism' (ODEs) but an unbounded plethora of qualitative phenomena as 'what-models,' lacks canonical formal intrinsic semantics, models time as continuous, and relies on paramount timescale hierarchies for organization.
solution: Jaeger conducts a wide-scope conceptual and theoretical survey of existing formalizations of "computing," rooted in logic-based (L), probabilistic (P), and dynamical systems-oriented (D) mathematical traditions.

The methodology involves a comparative analysis of four key theoretical themes across these domains: interrelationships between formalisms, formal intrinsic semantics, formal time, and hierarchical organization of constructs.

The paper proposes "Modal Computing" as a path forward, advocating for a shift from bi-stability to "modulatable dynamical modes" and from symbolic to "qualitative abstraction of modal nonlinear dynamics."
paper_summary.summary: Herbert Jaeger proposes a generalized concept of computing, termed "Modal Computing," to unify digital, neuromorphic, and unconventional paradigms by systematically analyzing their mathematical underpinnings. The work highlights fundamental differences in how these paradigms conceptualize time, semantics, and hierarchy, asserting that existing theoretical frameworks are insufficient for exploiting the full spectrum of physical phenomena for computation.

title: Exploring the landscapes of "computing": digital, neuromorphic,  unconventional -- and beyond

abstract: Cognitive Architectures are the forefront of the research into developing an artificial cognition. However, they approach the problem from a separated memory and program model of computation. This model of computation poses a fundamental problem: the knowledge retrieval heuristic. In this paper we propose to solve this problem by using a novel model of computation, one where memory and program are merged: the Function-Representation. This model of computation involves defining a generic Function-Representation and instantiating multiple instances of it. In this paper we explore the potential of this novel model of computation through mathematical definitions and proofs. We also explore the kind of functions a Function-Representation can implement, and present different ways to organise multiple instances of a Function-Representation.

keyInsights: Knowledge in a Function-Representation is directly accessed by applying its function, thus intrinsically solving the knowledge retrieval problem without external heuristics.
Non-linearity in an FR's base function is a necessary and sufficient condition for emergent behavior to arise from the connection of multiple FRs.
There is a fundamental trade-off between function types: associative functions preserve information but cannot abstract, while additive functions enable abstraction but inherently lose information.
originalProblem: Traditional Cognitive Architectures (CAs) face an intractable "knowledge retrieval heuristic problem" due to their separation of memory (representations) and program, making it difficult to efficiently retrieve contextually relevant information.
Existing CAs, despite diverse approaches, have not successfully produced true artificial cognition, suggesting a fundamental limitation in their underlying computational paradigm.
Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) implicitly merge memory and program, but lack a formal theoretical foundation explaining their success in this regard for broader cognitive applications.
results: It is formally proven that a Function-Representation contains knowledge if and only if its underlying function is not constant (Theorem 4).
Lemma 1 demonstrates that knowledge retrieval is eliminated in the FR model as knowledge is accessed directly by applying the function.

Theorems 5, 6, and 7 mathematically establish that emergent behavior from connected FRs occurs if and only if the FR's base function is non-linear, providing a theoretical basis for non-linear activation functions in ANNs.

Theorems 9, 10, 11, and 12 prove that additive functions cause information loss and cannot represent bijective functions, while associative functions preserve information but cannot represent non-bijective functions, highlighting fundamental design implications.
solution: The paper defines a Function-Representation (FR) as a function whose parameters constitute its knowledge representation, intrinsically merging memory and program.

It proposes a connectionist paradigm where complex behaviors emerge from the interaction of simple FRs, with self-organization and self-modification embedded within FRs to avoid global control algorithms.

The model categorizes FR functions into associative (bijective, preserving information) and additive (non-bijective, leading to information loss and abstraction) to define their computational properties.
paper_summary.summary: This paper introduces the Function-Representation Model of Computation, which merges memory and program into a single entity to resolve the long-standing knowledge retrieval problem in Cognitive Architectures. It mathematically proves that non-linearity is essential for emergent behavior and identifies fundamental trade-offs between information preservation and abstraction in function design.

title: The Function-Representation Model of Computation

abstract:
The fact that accurately predicted information can serve as an energy source
paves the way for new approaches to autonomous learning. The energy derived
from a sequence of successful predictions can be recycled as an immediate
incentive and resource, driving the enhancement of predictive capabilities in
AI agents. We propose that, through a series of straightforward
meta-architectural adjustments, any unsupervised learning apparatus could
achieve complete independence from external energy sources, evolving into a
self-sustaining physical system with a strong intrinsic 'drive' for continual
learning. This concept, while still purely theoretical, is exemplified through
the autoencoder, a quintessential model for unsupervised efficient coding. We
use this model to demonstrate how progressive paradigm shifts can profoundly
alter our comprehension of learning and intelligence. By reconceptualizing
learning as an energy-seeking process, we highlight the potential for achieving
true autonomy in learning systems, thereby bridging the gap between algorithmic
concepts and physical models of intelligence.
title:
The Physics of Learning: From Autoencoders to Truly Autonomous Learning
  Machines

abstract: The value of brain-inspired neuromorphic computers critically depends on our ability to program them for relevant tasks. Currently, neuromorphic hardware often relies on machine learning methods adapted from deep learning. However, neuromorphic computers have potential far beyond deep learning if we can only harness their energy efficiency and full computational power. Neuromorphic programming will necessarily be different from conventional programming, requiring a paradigm shift in how we think about programming. This paper presents a conceptual analysis of programming within the context of neuromorphic computing, challenging conventional paradigms and proposing a framework that aligns more closely with the physical intricacies of these systems. Our analysis revolves around five characteristics that are fundamental to neuromorphic programming and provides a basis for comparison to contemporary programming methods and languages. By studying past approaches, we contribute a framework that advocates for underutilized techniques and calls for richer abstractions to effectively instrument the new hardware class.
title: Neuromorphic Programming: Emerging Directions for Brain-Inspired Hardware

abstract: In this article, we review a class of neuro-mimetic computational models that we place under the label of spiking predictive coding. Specifically, we review the general framework of predictive processing in the context of neurons that emit discrete action potentials, i.e., spikes. Theoretically, we structure our survey around how prediction errors are represented, which results in an organization of historical neuromorphic generalizations that is centered around three broad classes of approaches: prediction errors in explicit groups of error neurons, in membrane potentials, and implicit prediction error encoding. Furthermore, we examine some applications of spiking predictive coding that utilize more energy-efficient, edge-computing hardware platforms. Finally, we highlight important future directions and challenges in this emerging line of inquiry in brain-inspired computing. Building on the prior results of work in computational cognitive neuroscience, machine intelligence, and neuromorphic engineering, we hope that this review of neuromorphic formulations and implementations of predictive coding will encourage and guide future research and development in this emerging research area.

keyInsights: No single class of spiking predictive coding models has proven definitively superior, with the most suitable approach depending on whether the goal is biological understanding or artificial system efficiency.
Energy efficiency, task formulation, and encoding/decoding mechanisms are fundamentally intertwined and should not be considered independently when developing brain-inspired AI.
Spiking predictive coding models operate at various spatial and cognitive levels, indicating different models may be appropriate for different scales of understanding the brain or building AI.
originalProblem: The literature at the intersection of predictive coding (PC) and spiking neural networks (SNNs) was fragmented and under-studied, lacking a unified framework.
Most existing PC implementations relied on rate-coded artificial neural networks, despite the biological plausibility and energy efficiency potential of SNNs.
Bridging the gap between the theoretical elegance of PC as a brain function theory and the practical advantages of SNNs for energy-efficient neuromorphic hardware was challenging.
results: A comprehensive taxonomy was established, classifying spiking predictive coding models into three main classes based on how prediction errors are represented: explicit error neurons, membrane potential as error, and implicit error encoding.

The survey synthesized findings on how each class addresses the challenge of representing signed prediction errors with intrinsically positive spiking activity and integrates various biophysical mechanisms and learning rules.

Neuromorphic applications of spiking PC were highlighted, including implementations on low-power microcontrollers for tasks like eye-blink conditioning and robotic pain models, demonstrating reduced neuron counts and power consumption for edge computing.
solution: The authors conducted a systematic review of neuro-mimetic computational models integrating predictive processing principles with spiking neural networks.
A novel unifying framework was proposed to organize the diverse literature, categorizing models by their method of representing prediction errors.
The survey examined existing applications of spiking predictive coding to neuromorphic hardware and identified open research questions and future directions for the field.
paper_summary.summary: This survey provides a structured overview of spiking neural network (SNN) models that implement predictive coding principles, termed 'spiking predictive coding,' organizing the fragmented literature into three distinct classes based on error representation. The work consolidates diverse approaches, ranging from models with explicit error neurons to those where prediction errors are implicitly encoded, and details their applications in energy-efficient neuromorphic computing.

title: Predictive Coding with Spiking Neural Networks: a Survey

abstract:
This paper envisions an end-to-end program generation scenario using
recurrent neural networks (RNNs): Users can express their intention in natural
language; an RNN then automatically generates corresponding code in a
characterby-by-character fashion. We demonstrate its feasibility through a case
study and empirical analysis. To fully make such technique useful in practice,
we also point out several cross-disciplinary challenges, including modeling
user intention, providing datasets, improving model architectures, etc.
Although much long-term research shall be addressed in this new field, we
believe end-to-end program generation would become a reality in future decades,
and we are looking forward to its practice.

author_info.institution: Full Professor of Computer Science, Peking University

title: On End-to-End Program Generation from User Intention by Deep Neural  Networks

abstract: In the realm of efficient on-device learning under extreme memory and computation constraints, a significant gap in successful approaches persists. Although considerable effort has been devoted to efficient inference, the main obstacle to efficient learning is the prohibitive cost of backpropagation. The resources required to compute gradients and update network parameters often exceed the limits of tightly constrained memory budgets. This paper challenges conventional wisdom and proposes a series of experiments that reveal the existence of superior sub-networks. Furthermore, we hint at the potential for substantial gains through a dynamic neuron selection strategy when fine-tuning a target task. Our efforts extend to the adaptation of a recent dynamic neuron selection strategy pioneered by Bragagnolo et al. (NEq), revealing its effectiveness in the most stringent scenarios. Our experiments demonstrate, in the average case, the superiority of a NEq-inspired approach over a random selection. This observation prompts a compelling avenue for further exploration in the area, highlighting the opportunity to design a new class of algorithms designed to facilitate parameter update selection. Our findings usher in a new era of possibilities in the field of on-device learning under extreme constraints and encourage the pursuit of innovative strategies for efficient, resource-friendly model fine-tuning.
title: Towards On-device Learning on the Edge: Ways to Select Neurons to Update under a Budget Constraint

abstract: A central goal in neuroscience is to provide explanations for how animal nervous systems can generate actions and cognitive states such as consciousness while artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) seek to provide models that are increasingly better at prediction. Despite many decades of research we have made limited progress on providing neuroscience explanations yet there is an increased use of AI and ML methods in neuroscience for prediction of behavior and even cognitive states. Here we propose emulator theory (ET) and neural emulators as circuit- and scale-independent predictive models of biological brain activity and emulator theory (ET) as an alternative research paradigm in neuroscience. ET proposes that predictive models trained solely on neural dynamics and behaviors can generate functionally indistinguishable systems from their sources. That is, compared to the biological organisms which they model, emulators may achieve indistinguishable behavior and cognitive states - including consciousness - without any mechanistic explanations. We posit ET via several conjectures, discuss the nature of endogenous and exogenous activation of neural circuits, and discuss neural causality of phenomenal states. ET provides the conceptual and empirical framework for prediction-based models of neural dynamics and behavior without explicit representations of idiosyncratically evolved nervous systems.
title: A theory of neural emulators

abstract:
To overcome the limitations of Neural Programmer-Interpreters (NPI) in its
universality and learnability, we propose the incorporation of combinator
abstraction into neural programing and a new NPI architecture to support this
abstraction, which we call Combinatory Neural Programmer-Interpreter (CNPI).
Combinator abstraction dramatically reduces the number and complexity of
programs that need to be interpreted by the core controller of CNPI, while
still allowing the CNPI to represent and interpret arbitrary complex programs
by the collaboration of the core with the other components. We propose a small
set of four combinators to capture the most pervasive programming patterns. Due
to the finiteness and simplicity of this combinator set and the offloading of
some burden of interpretation from the core, we are able construct a CNPI that
is universal with respect to the set of all combinatorizable programs, which is
adequate for solving most algorithmic tasks. Moreover, besides supervised
training on execution traces, CNPI can be trained by policy gradient
reinforcement learning with appropriately designed curricula.

organization_info.name: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications

title: Improving the Universality and Learnability of Neural  Programmer-Interpreters with Combinator Abstraction

abstract:
We introduce a neural network architecture and a learning algorithm to
produce factorized symbolic representations. We propose to learn these concepts
by observing consecutive frames, letting all the components of the hidden
representation except a small discrete set (gating units) be predicted from the
previous frame, and let the factors of variation in the next frame be
represented entirely by these discrete gated units (corresponding to symbolic
representations). We demonstrate the efficacy of our approach on datasets of
faces undergoing 3D transformations and Atari 2600 games.
title: Understanding Visual Concepts with Continuation Learning

abstract: We improve the results by Siegelmann & Sontag (1995) by providing a novel and parsimonious constructive mapping between Turing Machines and Recurrent Artificial Neural Networks, based on recent developments of Nonlinear Dynamical Automata. The architecture of the resulting R-ANNs is simple and elegant, stemming from its transparent relation with the underlying NDAs. These characteristics yield promise for developments in machine learning methods and symbolic computation with continuous time dynamical systems. A framework is provided to directly program the R-ANNs from Turing Machine descriptions, in absence of network training. At the same time, the network can potentially be trained to perform algorithmic tasks, with exciting possibilities in the integration of approaches akin to Google DeepMind's Neural Turing Machines.
title: Turing Computation with Recurrent Artificial Neural Networks

abstract: While foundation models have achieved remarkable results across a diversity of domains, they still rely on human-generated data, such as text, as a fundamental source of knowledge. However, this data is ultimately the product of human brains, the filtered projection of a deeper neural complexity. In this paper, we explore a new strategy for artificial intelligence: moving beyond surface-level statistical regularities by training foundation models directly on human brain data. We hypothesize that neuroimaging data could open a window into elements of human cognition that are not accessible through observable actions, and argue that this additional knowledge could be used, alongside classical training data, to overcome some of the current limitations of foundation models. While previous research has demonstrated the possibility to train classical machine learning or deep learning models on neural patterns, this path remains largely unexplored for high-level cognitive functions. Here, we classify the current limitations of foundation models, as well as the promising brain regions and cognitive processes that could be leveraged to address them, along four levels: perception, valuation, execution, and integration. Then, we propose two methods that could be implemented to prioritize the use of limited neuroimaging data for strategically chosen, high-value steps in foundation model training: reinforcement learning from human brain (RLHB) and chain of thought from human brain (CoTHB). We also discuss the potential implications for agents, artificial general intelligence, and artificial superintelligence, as well as the ethical, social, and technical challenges and opportunities. We argue that brain-trained foundation models could represent a realistic and effective middle ground between continuing to scale current architectures and exploring alternative, neuroscience-inspired solutions.
title: A New Strategy for Artificial Intelligence: Training Foundation Models Directly on Human Brain Data

abstract:
Modern computation based on the von Neumann architecture is today a mature
cutting-edge science. In the Von Neumann architecture, processing and memory
units are implemented as separate blocks interchanging data intensively and
continuously. This data transfer is responsible for a large part of the power
consumption. The next generation computer technology is expected to solve
problems at the exascale with 1018 calculations each second. Even though these
future computers will be incredibly powerful, if they are based on von Neumann
type architectures, they will consume between 20 and 30 megawatts of power and
will not have intrinsic physically built-in capabilities to learn or deal with
complex data as our brain does. These needs can be addressed by neuromorphic
computing systems which are inspired by the biological concepts of the human
brain. This new generation of computers has the potential to be used for the
storage and processing of large amounts of digital information with much lower
power consumption than conventional processors. Among their potential future
applications, an important niche is moving the control from data centers to
edge devices.
  The aim of this Roadmap is to present a snapshot of the present state of
neuromorphic technology and provide an opinion on the challenges and
opportunities that the future holds in the major areas of neuromorphic
technology, namely materials, devices, neuromorphic circuits, neuromorphic
algorithms, applications, and ethics. The Roadmap is a collection of
perspectives where leading researchers in the neuromorphic community provide
their own view about the current state and the future challenges. We hope that
this Roadmap will be a useful resource to readers outside this field, for those
who are just entering the field, and for those who are well established in the
neuromorphic community. https://doi.org/10.1088/2634-4386/ac4a83

organization_info.name: King Abdullah University of Science and Technology (KAUST)
INRiM - Istituto Nazionale di Ricerca Metrologica

title: 2022 Roadmap on Neuromorphic Computing and Engineering

abstract: Neuromorphic Computing promises orders of magnitude improvement in energy efficiency compared to traditional von Neumann computing paradigm. The goal is to develop an adaptive, fault-tolerant, low-footprint, fast, low-energy intelligent system by learning and emulating brain functionality which can be realized through innovation in different abstraction layers including material, device, circuit, architecture and algorithm. As the energy consumption in complex vision tasks keep increasing exponentially due to larger data set and resource-constrained edge devices become increasingly ubiquitous, spike-based neuromorphic computing approaches can be viable alternative to deep convolutional neural network that is dominating the vision field today. In this book chapter, we introduce neuromorphic computing, outline a few representative examples from different layers of the design stack (devices, circuits and algorithms) and conclude with a few exciting applications and future research directions that seem promising for computer vision in the near future.
